steps.title: Identify the core disagreement between the texts
steps.content:
Fowler (Text 1) claims mandatory voting makes election results better reflect the preferences of the whole country. Singh and Roy (Text 2) counter that forced voters spend less time researching candidates, so their votes may not reflect their actual preferences. Singh and Roy's response must directly challenge Fowler's specific claim.

Now check each choice against that disagreement:
  • Choice A — Only countries of a certain population size should implement mandatory voting.: Neither text mentions population size as a factor in mandatory voting outcomes. Unsupported. ✗
  • Choice B — People who are forced to vote are likely to become politically engaged in other ways, such as volunteering or running for office.: Neither text discusses other forms of civic engagement beyond voting. Unsupported. ✗
  • Choice C — Requiring people to vote does not necessarily lead to election outcomes that better represent the preferences of the country as a whole.: This is the direct negation of Fowler's claim. Singh and Roy argue that forced voters cast less-informed votes that may not reflect their preferences, undermining the representativeness Fowler asserts. ✓
  • Choice D — Countries that require voting must also make the process of voting easier for their citizens.: Neither text discusses ease or difficulty of the voting process. Unsupported. ✗

steps.title: Confirm the answer
steps.content: Singh and Roy's finding that forced votes may not reflect actual preferences directly refutes Fowler's claim about representativeness. That matches Choice C — Requiring people to vote does not necessarily lead to election outcomes that better represent the preferences of the country as a whole..
